txt_distinct_choirs: Yes, most of the choirs fill different needs in the choral scene. They have different vibes for sure. When Polycoro started, though, I definitely thought, “okay, Winnipeg doesn’t need another choir…”

Historically Canzona has had the most clear identity as a baroque ensemble.

Winnipeg Singers fills the roll of a traditional chamber choir, singing a combination a mainstream historical music, and modern compositions.

Dead of winter sings a lot of indigenous music, which is a critically important voice in our choral climate. While they also sing a early music concert.

Polycoro distinguished itself primarily through the addition of multi media at their concerts. Their programming generally over laps with, or is on the fringe, of programing you could expect from dead of winter or Canzona. They do strive for a exceptional level of excellence with a focus on small ensemble singing.

repetoire:
Baroque and renaissance period music. 

Winnipeg doesn't see much of the latter in particular, and I find soaring polyphonic works to be some of the most beautiful choral music (Byrd, tallis, Victoria, etc.). It's not all sacred, but many of the slow ones are. Rossi has plenty of Jewish stuff if you want a break from the Christian. 

A Capella is great for such rep, which can save a bit on instrumentalists. Though there are also plenty of works that call for them if the budget is there. Bonus points if period accurate - lute, theorbo, baroque strings, etc.). Something modern in that style that could be fun is elam rotem's lamentations of david

I do also enjoy classic masterworks. The requiem, masses, etc. Might be interesting to hear one of the other completions of Mozart's requiem, for example. 

Unrelated, but I've always been interested in hearing some east orthodox slavonic style music (ex: Tchaikovsky's cherbubic hymn, which is part of a larger work). Do we have any oktavists in winnipeg? 

Would be especially nice to hear such music in stone venues like the legislature or the train station. Same thing for slow polyphonic works as mentioned above.
what_would_increase_attendance: Rep I'm excited about. Period accurate instruments. Straight(er) - tone singing. Stone venues (if appropriate).

repetoire: Classical. I can sometimes find interest in new classical works, but not always. If there is too much new, I do not go.
txt_distinct_choirs: Because of their nature, I am much more likely to attend Canzona and the Phil than Dead of Winter, which is too trendy and too woke for me. The only Dead of Winter concerts I attend are the Renaissance ones. I like the Winnipeg Singers because of their tremendous quality, but will not attend a concert of all Canadian music. I think they are limited by the repertoire they are obliged to sing. Canzona I have underattended in the past, not because of repertoire, but because of lack of time. I am lukewarm on Proximus 5, which is a small group. I prefer a bigger sound.

repetoire: Everything. Renaissance, Baroque, and contemporary works that open up new sounds and present interesting ideas and meaningful messages are the types that I find most interesting. I prefer truthful repertoire that doesn’t pander to societal trends, as much as that’s what sells nowadays.
what_would_increase_attendance: Nothing in particular. Maybe better central concert venues that are beautiful and suitable for acoustic choral performance. Can someone find money to build or convert one?! (Being busy and with young kids is the reason I am attending fewer concerts these days, by the way.)
txt_distinct_choirs: DoW seems to promote contemporary Indigenous composers and artists, including Andrew of course, and they still have a foot in the early music domain; Winnipeg Singers seems to program accessible, mostly-contemporary works; Polycoro seems to program a variety of Renaissance and cutting edge contemporary works that are rarely performed, and presents their concerts in evocative ways; Canzona covers the Baroque repertoire/masterworks (more contemporary variety as of late but with an outgoing AD), and Proximus 5 performs one-per-part a cappella in a more intimate manner on the whole.
received_from_other: Note that I attend many options on the previous page but could only select one option, so I chose “other live performances”
